narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away feels like a door slamming shut just as you're reaching for the handle. Our prediction model looked at your profile and estimated a 43.8% chance you'd struggle to pass the bar exam, which is why we had to make this tough call. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 2, placing you in the lower ranks of your school's grades that year, signaling to the model a steeper uphill battle ahead in law's demanding final stretch. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 2, painting a picture of early challenges that didn't shift much over time, the kind that often foreshadows bar exam hurdles. Your LSAT score of 35 added to that concern, as it fell short of the benchmarks where others pull ahead on high-stakes tests like the bar. With your middle family income, the model saw less of a safety net for the intense prep many need, nudging the outlook toward struggle. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.1 reinforced this, suggesting academic momentum that's solid but not quite bar-passing caliber in our past patterns. Being a white male in a full-time program didn't tip the scales your way either, as those traits have historically aligned with tougher odds in similar profiles. It's this combination—like low deciles bookending your record, paired with that LSAT and GPA—that built the model's story of risk, even if you're capable of more. You've got target_law drive burning bright at full strength, and that's no small thing. This isn't the end of your story; think of it as a pivot point to build those deciles higher or boost that LSAT through retakes. Law needs determined minds like yours—keep pushing, and the right door will open.
